<domain>math</domain>
<title>A ccc indestructible construction with CH</title>
<url>https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24821v1</url>
<abstract>We introduce a variant of the Kurepa family. We then use one such family to construct a ccc indestructible property associated with a complete coherent Suslin tree $S$. Moreover, in every ccc forcing extension that preserves Suslin of $S$, forcing with $S$ induces a strong negative partition relation.</abstract>
output:
<think>
Okay, let me try to work through this. The user wants me to create an experiment design based on the given paper. The paper is titled "A ccc indestructible construction with CH" and it's in the math domain. The abstract mentions Kurepa families, complete coherent Suslin trees, and ccc forcing extensions. 

First, I need to understand the research question here. The paper seems to be about constructing a ccc indestructible property related to a Suslin tree under the Continuum Hypothesis (CH). The hypotheses might involve whether certain properties hold under specific forcing conditions. 

The dataset choice is tricky because the paper uses mathematical structures like Suslin trees and Kurepa families, which aren't typical datasets. Since the paper doesn't mention using real-world data, maybe the dataset is synthetic. The description should explain that it's synthetic, generated based on the concepts in the paper. Access would be through theoretical models or code that simulates these structures.

Metrics: The primary metric could be the success rate of preserving the Suslin tree property under forcing. Secondary metrics might include the induction of negative partition relations. 

Baselines: Weak baselines could be standard forcing methods without the indestructible property. Strong baselines might involve existing constructions related to Suslin trees and Kurepa families.

Procedure steps: First, generate synthetic Suslin trees and Kurepa families. Then, simulate ccc forcing extensions. Test if the constructed property holds. Measure the metrics. Compare with baselines.

input:
<domain>math</domain>
<title>Number of $K$-rational points with given $j$-invariant on modular curves</title>
<url>https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24817v1</url>
<abstract>In this article, we study how to compute the number of $K$-rational points with a given $j$-invariant on an arbitrary modular curve. As an application, for each positive integer $n$, we determine the list of possible numbers of cyclic $n$-isogenies an elliptic curve over some number field can admit. Similarly, for an odd prime power $p^k$, we calculate the possible values for the number of points above some $j$-invariant on Cartan modular curves $X_{\mathrm s}(p^k)$, $X_{\mathrm{ns}}(p^k)$ and their normalizers.   Combining known results about images of Galois representations of CM elliptic curves with our work, we also devise a simple algorithm to determine the number of rational CM points on any modular curve.</abstract>

<domain>math</domain>
<title>Four collapsing one-dimensional particles: a dynamical system approach of the spherical billiard reduction</title>
<url>https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24812v1</url>
<abstract>We consider a system of four one-dimensional inelastic hard spheres evolving on the real line $\mathbb{R}$, and colliding according to a scattering law characterized by a fixed restitution coefficient $r$. We study the possible orders of collisions when the inelastic collapse occurs, relying on the so-called $\mathfrak{b}$-to-$\mathfrak{b}$ mapping, a two-dimensional dynamical system associated to the original particle system which encodes all the possible collision orders. We prove that the $\mathfrak{b}$-to-$\mathfrak{b}$ mapping is a piecewise projective transformation, which allows one to perform efficient numerical simulations of its orbits. We recover previously known results concerning the one-dimensional four-particle inelastic hard sphere system and we support the conjectures stated in the literature concerning particular periodic orbits. We discover three new families of periodic orbits that coexist depending on the restitution coefficient, we prove rigorously that there exist stable periodic orbits for the $\mathfrak{b}$-to-$\mathfrak{b}$ mapping for restitution coefficients larger than the upper bounds previously known, and we prove the existence of quasi-periodic orbits for this mapping.</abstract>

<domain>math</domain>
<title>Twisted Cherednik systems and non-symmetric Macdonald polynomials</title>
<url>https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24811v2</url>
<abstract>We consider eigenfunctions of many-body system Hamiltonians associated with generalized (a-twisted) Cherednik operators used in construction of other Hamiltonians: those arising from commutative subalgebras of the Ding-Iohara-Miki (DIM) algebra. The simplest example of these eigenfunctions is provided by non-symmetric Macdonald polynomials, while generally they are constructed basing on the ground state eigenfunction coinciding with the twisted Baker-Akhiezer function being a peculiar (symmetric) eigenfunction of the DIM Hamiltonians. Moreover, the eigenfunctions admit an expansion with universal coefficients so that the dependence on the twist $a$ is hidden only in these ground state eigenfunctions, and we suggest a general formula that allows one to construct these eigenfunctions from non-symmetric Macdonald polynomials. This gives a new twist in theory of integrable systems, which usually puts an accent on symmetric polynomials, and provides a new dimension to the {\it triad} made from the symmetric Macdonald polynomials, untwisted Baker-Akhiezer functions and Noumi-Shiraishi series.</abstract>

There's talk about twisting parameters and how eigenfunctions can be constructed from these polynomials. The paper also references integrable systems and the triad involving symmetric polynomials, Baker-Akhiezer functions, and Noumi-Shiraishi series.

So the research question here is probably about verifying the relationship between twisted Cherednik operators, non-symmetric Macdonald polynomials, and their eigenfunctions. The hypotheses might involve the role of the twist parameter 'a' and the structure of eigenfunctions derived from these polynomials.

input:
<domain>math</domain>
<title>Hölder continuity of weak solutions to the thin-film equation in $d=2$</title>
<url>https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24809v1</url>
<abstract>The thin-film equation $\partial_t u = -\nabla \cdot (u^n \nabla Δu)$ describes the evolution of the height $u=u(x,t)\geq 0$ of a viscous thin liquid film spreading on a flat solid surface. We prove Hölder continuity of energy-dissipating weak solutions to the thin-film equation in the physically most relevant case of two spatial dimensions $d=2$. While an extensive existence theory of weak solutions to the thin-film equation was established more than two decades ago, even boundedness of weak solutions in $d=2$ has remained a major unsolved problem in the theory of the thin-film equation. Due the fourth-order structure of the thin-film equation, De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theory is not applicable. Our proof is based on the hole-filling technique, the challenge being posed by the degenerate parabolicity of the fourth-order PDE.</abstract>

The hypotheses would be that the weak solutions are Hölder continuous, which aligns with the paper's main contribution. But since the paper already proved it, maybe the hypotheses here are about confirming the conditions under which this holds, like the parameters n and d=2.

For the dataset, the paper is theoretical, so synthetic data might be better. The description should mention generating initial conditions that satisfy the equation's properties, like non-negative solutions. Access would be through simulation or mathematical generation.

Baselines: Weak solutions with lower regularity, maybe using different numerical methods to compare. Strong baselines could be known results from other studies.

Short analysis: If the synthetic data matches the paper's findings, then the hypotheses hold.

Limitations: Real-world data might not fit the model, next steps could involve testing in higher dimensions or different n values.
</think>
